message.content:
## Web Searcher: practitioner/implementation — Apple Silicon local inference

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Your angle: **practitioner/implementation — Apple Silicon local inference** — Targets RTF, memory, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and Python integration effort for swapping into the existing Kokoro pipeline (Angle 5).
Search query: `run TTS locally Apple Silicon M-series MPS CoreML ONNX real-time factor RAM Kokoro Piper StyleTTS2 F5-TTS Python pipeline integration`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Kokoro TTS performance on Apple Silicon:

## Performance Benchmarks

An M1 Mac Mini with 16 GB of RAM synthesizes 30 seconds of speech in under two seconds (15x realtime), and performance ranges from 12-79x realtime across the lineup, with the M2 Ultra finishing 30s of audio in 379 ms (79x RT).

A separate benchmark found that Kokoro is the fastest (RTF 0.17) at only 170 MB when compared against other TTS engines.

## Implementation Approaches

There are multiple ways to run Kokoro on Apple Silicon:

1. **CoreML Pipeline**: Swift+CoreML eliminates Python overhead by running models on the ANE with no fallback, using native Swift with no Python at inference.

2. **MLX Framework**: Kokoro TTS inference runs on Apple Silicon via MLX with an implementation that has no PyTorch or transformers dependency.

3. **Local Deployment**: Kokoro-82M runs comfortably on a standard NVIDIA GPU or even Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3) via MPS acceleration.

## Key Advantages

Every Mac with Apple Silicon is a TTS server, enabling fully local inference without cloud dependencies.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of running TTS locally on Apple Silicon with various frameworks and models:

## Local TTS on Apple Silicon M-series

### Performance & Real-Time Factor

Kokoro runs comfortably faster than real-time on Apple Silicon CPU — community tests on M-series chips report anywhere from roughly 5x up to the teens times real time depending on the machine. More specifically, an M1 Mac Mini with 16 GB of RAM synthesizes 30 seconds of speech in under two seconds, which is 15x realtime. Across the M-series lineup, speeds range from 12-79x realtime, with the M2 Ultra finishing 30s of audio in 379 ms (79x RT).

### Model Comparison

**Kokoro (82M parameters)**
- Kokoro-82M is an Apache-2.0 text-to-speech model with just 82M parameters that runs fast on CPU
- Kokoro TTS is an emerging alternative to Piper in the lightweight category and achieves higher naturalness than Piper while remaining fast on CPU
- On an M1 MacBook Air with 8GB of RAM, Kokoro generates a 1,500-word passage in roughly 30 to 45 seconds, and on M2 Pro or M3 hardware, it is noticeably faster

**Piper**
- Apple Silicon achieves approximately 15× real-time on M5 Pro (CPU, ARM NEON), running natively without GPU

**F5-TTS & StyleTTS2**
- Kokoro's architecture is based on the work of StyleTTS2

### Framework Options

**MLX Framework**
- MLX-Audio is a speech library built on Apple's MLX framework and optimized for Apple Silicon machines (M-series Macs), focusing on text-to-speech and speech-to-speech workflows, and because it uses MLX and targets Apple Silicon, inference is fast and can take advantage of hardware acceleration and quantization for efficient on-device performance

**CoreML Conversion**
- A PyTorch → CoreML conversion pipeline for Kokoro TTS unlocks fast on-device text-to-speech on Apple Neural Engine
- Swift+CoreML eliminates Python bottlenecks—models run on the ANE with no fallback, and orchestration is native Swift with no Python

**ONNX Runtime**
- A production-tested architecture for fully local voice AI on Apple Silicon uses speech-to-text via Whisper.cpp with Metal GPU acceleration, inference via Ollama, and text-to-speech via Kokoro ONNX with a persistent HTTP server

### RAM Requirements

Kokoro-82M has Apache-2.0 licensing, approximately 327MB weights, 54 voices across 8 languages, and runs fast on CPU. Kokoro is the efficiency champion: 82M parameters, 8 languages, 54 baked-in voices, and it'll do 36x real-time on a free Colab T4 or 5x on a 32-core CPU.

### Python Pipeline Integration

MLX-Audio provides a straightforward CLI as well as a Python API for programmatic generation of audio, including parameters for voice choice, speed, language hints, output format, and sample rate. An MLX implementation of the Kokoro-82M text-to-speech pipeline includes inference code with no PyTorch or transformers dependency.

### Cross-Platform Considerations

A deployment pipeline where a single distilled ONNX model produced consistent TTS output on all three target platforms, validated against a single quality baseline rather than three separate ones, with latency on the lowest-specification test devices within the real-time synthesis budget.

Based on the search results, here's what I found:

MLX Audio is a premier audio library built on Apple's MLX framework, delivering high-performance text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-text (STT), and speech-to-speech (STS) on M-series chips.

The library supports several TTS models including Kokoro, Qwen3-TTS, Voxtral TTS, CSM, Dia, and more. Specifically:

- **Kokoro**: Kokoro is a fast, multilingual TTS model with 54 voice presets.
- **Dia**: Available alongside Qwen3-TTS and Kokoro in one browser UI.

The project provides a straightforward CLI (mlx_audio.tts.generate) as well as a Python API for programmatic generation of audio, including parameters for voice choice, speed, language hints, output format, and sample rate.

Regarding performance metrics, streaming chunks include PCM samples and timing metrics including RTF (real-time factor).

However, the search results don't contain specific benchmark data comparing RTF (Real-Time Factor) across these models. For detailed performance benchmarks between Kokoro, Dia, and other models on Apple Silicon, you may want to check the official MLX Audio documentation or GitHub repositories directly.

I found search results related to your query. Here's what I discovered about these TTS models and their performance characteristics:

## VRAM Requirements

Chatterbox and F5-TTS need 2–3 GB of VRAM for inference, making them relatively lightweight models compared to others in the field.

## Apple Silicon (MPS) Support

Chatterbox's Apple Silicon/MPS support was recently fixed for the Turbo model by forcing float32 in s3tokenizer and voice_encoder, resolving a crash issue with MPS Tensor conversion.

## Real-Time Factor and Inference Speed

GigaGPU benchmarks compare first-audio latency and real-time factor (RTF) for Kokoro, XTTS-v2, Bark, F5-TTS, Chatterbox, Piper and more. However, specific RTF values for Apple Silicon MPS are not prominently featured in these results. F5-TTS requires approximately 3 GB of VRAM for model + activations, and CPU-only works but is slow.

Chatterbox Turbo is designed with a 350M-parameter architecture to use less compute/VRAM while keeping high-fidelity output, with the audio diffusion decoder distilled from 10 steps to 1 step.

The search results indicate there are benchmark repositories available (like tts-bench on GitHub), but specific 2026 benchmark data comparing all three models' real-time factors specifically on Apple Silicon MPS with VRAM measurements wasn't directly provided in these results. You may want to check the tts-bench GitHub repository for the latest hardware-specific benchmark data.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# Local TTS Models for Mac 2026: Key Findings

Based on the Murmur guide, here are the tested models with practical specs:

## Models Covered

**Kokoro**
- Size: 82M parameters
- Speed: 30-45 seconds for 1,500 words on M1
- Memory: ~2GB
- Languages: 9
- Voice cloning: Yes (10-second sample)

**Qwen3 TTS**
- Size: ~500M parameters
- Speed: 60-90 seconds for 1,500 words
- Memory: ~4GB
- Languages: 15+
- Voice cloning: Yes (voice design capability)
- Strength: Code-switching between languages

**Fish Audio S2 Pro**
- Size: ~600M parameters
- Speed: 2-3 minutes for 1,500 words
- Memory: ~6GB
- Languages: 8
- Voice cloning: Yes (10-second sample)

**Chatterbox Turbo**
- Size: ~300M parameters
- Speed: ~60 seconds for 1,500 words
- Memory: ~3GB
- Languages: 6
- Voice cloning: Yes (10-second sample)

## Key Limitation

The source material does **not address** timestamps, ONNX/MLX/CoreML support, or Python integration specifics. It focuses on user experience rather than technical infrastructure details needed for detailed integration work.

# TTS Benchmark Results for Apple Silicon

Based on the Soniqo benchmarks page, here are the TTS performance metrics:

## Kokoro Performance
**Model:** Kokoro-82M CoreML
- **RTF:** 0.17 (fastest among tested engines)
- **Model Size:** ~170 MB
- **Round-trip WER:** 3.90%
- **Key advantage:** "Kokoro is the fastest (RTF 0.17) at only 170 MB"

## Comparative TTS Results
Testing used 30 English conversational sentences on Apple Silicon:

| Engine | Model | RTF | Memory | WER% |
|--------|-------|-----|--------|------|
| CosyVoice3 | 0.5B 4-bit | 0.59 | ~1.9 GB | 3.25 |
| Qwen3-TTS | 1.7B 8-bit | 0.85 | ~3.5 GB | 3.66 |
| Kokoro-82M | CoreML | 0.17 | ~170 MB | 3.90 |

## Hardware & Framework
- **Chip tested:** Apple M5 Pro (48 GB, macOS 25.5)
- **Framework:** CoreML (Kokoro); MLX for larger models
- **Overall finding:** "All TTS engines run faster than real-time (RTF < 1.0)"

The benchmarks show Kokoro excels in speed and memory efficiency, though CosyVoice3 achieved superior intelligibility scores.

content.content:
# Kokoro TTS on Apple Silicon: Practical Integration Details

## Integration Architecture

Kokoro is deployed as a **persistent HTTP server** rather than per-call CLI invocation. The article demonstrates this with a minimal Python implementation that loads the ONNX model at startup and serves synthesis requests on port 8181. This architecture eliminates repeated model loading overhead.

## Performance & Latency

The most striking metric: **~9 seconds reduced to ~300ms** through two optimizations:

1. **ONNX Runtime over PyTorch**: "ONNX Runtime on ARM64 uses optimized NEON SIMD instructions. 4–10x faster for a model this size."

2. **Persistent process model**: Loading from disk takes 1–2 seconds; keeping the model in memory eliminates this per-utterance.

Warm TTS latency benchmarks on M3 Pro:
- **Kokoro ONNX server**: ~200–500ms
- **Full voice loop** (record → transcribe → LLM → TTS): ~1.5–2.5 seconds

## Resource Usage

- **Disk footprint**: ~500MB total (Whisper tiny + Kokoro ONNX + voices + Python venv)
- **RAM**: Model stays resident in memory; the article targets M3 Pro with 36GB but notes "scales down to M1/8GB with smaller models"
- **Hardware acceleration**: Uses system resources available; no explicit MPS (Metal Performance Shaders) discussion, but Whisper.cpp auto-detects Metal GPU

## Integration Method

The server accepts POST requests with JSON payload:
```python
{"text": "...", "voice": "am_puck", "speed": 1.0}
```

Voice options include 26 styles (American, British English variants). Recommended voice: "am_puck — sharp, expressive, good for conversational AI."

## Engine Swapping

No alternative TTS engines are discussed in the article.

content.text: I now have comprehensive practitioner-focused data covering RTF, RAM, MPS/CoreML/ONNX/MLX support, latency, and Python integration. Let me compile the top results ranked by relevance to the original question.

results.title: Apple Silicon Speech Benchmarks — Whisper, Kokoro, Parakeet, Qwen3-ASR (WER & RTF on M-series) — Soniqo
results.snippet: The single most useful practitioner source: independent, reproducible RTF + memory + WER benchmarks measured ON Apple Silicon (M5 Pro, 48GB, macOS 25.5). Concrete head-to-head numbers — Kokoro-82M (CoreML): RTF 0.17, ~170MB RAM, 3.90% round-trip WER; CosyVoice3 0.5B 4-bit: RTF 0.59, ~1.9GB, 3.25% WER; Qwen3-TTS 1.7B 8-bit: RTF 0.85, ~3.5GB, 3.66% WER. Directly answers Angle 5: Kokoro is the fastest and lightest local option on Mac; heavier models give modestly better intelligibility at 3-20x the RAM and 3-5x the RTF. Notes Kokoro runs via CoreML, larger models via MLX. Caveat: vendor-affiliated (Soniqo sells Mac speech SDK), but the methodology is stated.

results.title: Building a Local Voice AI Stack: Whisper + Ollama + Kokoro TTS on Apple Silicon — DEV Community
results.snippet: Best source for actual Python integration effort and the swap pattern the pipeline needs. Shows Kokoro served as a persistent ONNX HTTP server (port 8181) with a simple POST {text, voice, speed} JSON API — directly maps to replacing a Python call. Key practitioner findings: ONNX Runtime on ARM64 (NEON SIMD) is 4-10x faster than PyTorch for a model this size; persistent-process model drops latency from ~9s (cold/CLI) to ~200-500ms warm on M3 Pro; ~500MB total disk footprint; explicitly notes it 'scales down to M1/8GB'. Real-world latency budget: full record→transcribe→LLM→TTS loop ~1.5-2.5s. Confirms 26 American/British voices.

results.title: Blaizzy/mlx-audio — TTS/STT/STS library built on Apple's MLX framework
results.snippet: The reference MLX path for Apple Silicon and the most plausible drop-in upgrade layer for a Kokoro Python pipeline. Provides both a CLI (mlx_audio.tts.generate) and a Python API (voice, speed, language, sample rate, output format) and supports Kokoro PLUS heavier candidates under one API: Qwen3-TTS, CSM/Sesame, Dia, Voxtral. Crucially for Angle 2 (timing): streaming chunks emit PCM + timing metrics including RTF. Uses MLX hardware acceleration + quantization on M-series; Kokoro MLX implementation has no PyTorch/transformers dependency. Lets you keep Kokoro and add bigger voices behind the same call interface.

results.title: Best Local TTS Models for Mac: Tested 2026 Picks — Murmur
results.snippet: Practical Mac-specific render-speed and RAM table for the leading 2026 candidates, useful for sizing the swap. On M1: Kokoro 82M ~2GB RAM, 30-45s per 1,500 words; Qwen3-TTS ~500M, ~4GB, 60-90s; Chatterbox Turbo ~300M, ~3GB, ~60s; Fish Audio S2 ~600M, ~6GB, 2-3min. Confirms Kokoro is the throughput/memory leader and the heavier voice-cloning models cost 2-6x RAM and 2-4x render time. Weakness flagged: does NOT cover timestamps, ONNX/MLX/CoreML support, or Python integration — UX-oriented, and a vendor blog, so treat render times as indicative.

results.title: mattmireles/kokoro-coreml — PyTorch → CoreML conversion pipeline for Kokoro TTS
results.snippet: Directly relevant to maximizing local-inference practicality: converts Kokoro to CoreML to run on the Apple Neural Engine. Claims 'Swift+CoreML eliminates Python bottlenecks — models run on the ANE with no fallback.' This is the highest-performance Apple-native path (matches the RTF 0.17 CoreML figure from the Soniqo benchmark) but increases integration effort because it pushes you off the Python pipeline toward Swift/native — a tradeoff worth flagging for a pipeline that today calls Kokoro from Python (ONNX-via-Python may be the better-effort choice; CoreML/ANE the better-perf choice).

results.title: TTS Latency Benchmarks — GigaGPU (Kokoro, XTTS-v2, F5-TTS, Chatterbox, Piper, Bark)
results.snippet: Cross-model first-audio latency and RTF comparison covering the full candidate set (Kokoro, XTTS-v2, F5-TTS, Chatterbox, Piper, Bark). Useful context for relative model cost, and corroborates that flow-matching/diffusion models (F5-TTS, Chatterbox) need ~2-3GB VRAM and are CPU-slow versus lightweight Kokoro/Piper. Caveat for this angle: numbers are GPU-cloud oriented rather than measured on Apple Silicon MPS, so use it for relative ranking of models, not for absolute Mac RTF — pair it with the Soniqo M5 Pro numbers.

content.text:
I've completed the research on the Apple Silicon local-inference practitioner angle. The top sources are ranked and returned via StructuredOutput.

Key practitioner takeaways for the original question (Angle 5 — local-inference practicality on Apple Silicon):

- **Kokoro is the baseline to beat and very hard to beat on practicality.** Independently measured on M5 Pro: RTF 0.17, ~170MB RAM (CoreML) — the fastest and lightest of the tested engines. On M1 it does ~30-45s per 1,500 words at ~2GB.
- **Best low-effort integration path:** keep Kokoro served as a persistent ONNX HTTP server in Python (POST {text, voice, speed}). ONNX Runtime on ARM64 (NEON SIMD) is 4-10x faster than PyTorch here, and the persistent-process model takes warm latency from ~9s down to ~200-500ms on M3 Pro.
- **Best Python upgrade layer:** Blaizzy/mlx-audio gives a unified Python API across Kokoro + heavier candidates (Qwen3-TTS, CSM/Sesame, Dia) on MLX, and notably emits RTF/timing metrics on streaming chunks (relevant to the alignment angle).
- **Highest absolute perf path:** kokoro-coreml pushes inference onto the Apple Neural Engine but moves you off Python toward Swift — a perf-vs-effort tradeoff to flag.
- **Heavier models cost a lot more locally:** F5-TTS/Chatterbox need ~2-3GB VRAM and are CPU-slow; CosyVoice3/Qwen3-TTS reach RTF 0.59-0.85 and 1.9-3.5GB RAM for only modest WER gains (3.25-3.66% vs Kokoro 3.90%).

One caveat flagged in the output: Soniqo and Murmur are vendor-affiliated, and the GigaGPU numbers are GPU-cloud rather than measured on MPS — so they're best used for relative ranking, paired with the M5 Pro CoreML figures for absolute Mac numbers.
